coredata.dc:title: Facile loading carbon dots on Co3O4 as an enhanced oxygen reduction reaction catalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoyan
Cheng, Xiumei
Chen, Feng
Xu, Li
Wang, Yaping
Qian, Junchao
Wu, Zhengying
Zhang, Qi
coredata.dc:description: One of the crucial issues in fuel cells is to explore the highly-efficient, long-term stable and low-cost catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Here, the carbon dots/Co3O4 (CDs/Co3O4) is reported as an effective electrocatalyst for ORR. The CDs/Co3O4 was synthesized through an in-situ two-step heating strategy with the graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and organic base as raw materials. The carbon dots (CDs) are derived from the C source of g-C3N4, which are in situ introduced into the Co3O4 to form the CDs/Co3O4 composite. Consequently, the introduction of CDs endows enhanced electrocatalytic activity of the CDs/Co3O4 composite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Carbon dots
Co3O4
Oxygen reduction reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra (A) and FT-IR spectra (B) of CDs/Co3O4 (a), pure Co3O4 (b) and g-C3N4 (c); XPS spectra of CDs/Co3O4 (a) and pure Co3O4 (b): (C) Survey, (D) high resolution XPS of Co 2p, (E) high resolution XPS of O 1s and (F) N 1s high resolution XPS of CDs/Co3O4.
SEM images of Co3O4 (A) and CDs/Co3O4 (B); The TEM images of Co3O4 (C, D) and CDs/Co3O4 (E, F).
(A) Raman spectra of CDs/Co3O4 and the corresponding N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms with BJH pore size distribution curve (inset).

Cyclic voltammetry results of CDs/Co3O4 (A) and Co3O4 (B) under N2 or O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte with a scan rate of 50 mV/s; (C) Linear sweep voltammetry results of CDs/Co3O4 and Co3O4 under O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte with a scan rate of 10 mV/s; (D) Linear sweep voltammetry results of CDs/Co3O4 at different rotation speeds of 400, 625, 900, 1225, 1600, 2025 and 2500 rpm. The electrochemical performances of CDs/Co3O4: (e) RRDE test at 1600 rpm; (f) H2O2 (%) yield and electron transfer number (n); (g) Methanol tolerance test; (h) The current–time (i-t) chronoamperometric response test.

head.ce:title: Facile loading carbon dots on Co3O4 as an enhanced oxygen reduction reaction catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Key Laboratory of Zhenjiang, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: iHuman Institute, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: SEM images of Co3O4 (A) and CDs/Co3O4 (B); The TEM images of Co3O4 (C, D) and CDs/Co3O4 (E, F).

One of the crucial issues in fuel cells is to explore the highly-efficient, long-term stable and low-cost catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Here, the carbon dots/Co3O4 (CDs/Co3O4) is reported as an effective electrocatalyst for ORR. The CDs/Co3O4 was synthesized through an in-situ two-step heating strategy with the graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and organic base as raw materials. The carbon dots (CDs) are derived from the C source of g-C3N4, which are in situ introduced into the Co3O4 to form the CDs/Co3O4 composite. Consequently, the introduction of CDs endows enhanced electrocatalytic activity of the CDs/Co3O4 composite.

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel cells are considered as the promising alternative to energy and environment challenges because the chemical energy can be directly converted into the electrical energy through fuel cells [1]. For fuel cells, the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is a very important reaction in the cathode. However, the practical energy density and long-term cycling activity of the fuel cell are severely hampered by slow sluggish kinetics for ORR. Therefore, ORR catalysts with efficiency, long-term stability and long cycle life are extremely important for fuel cells. Up to now, Pt-based materials are utilized as the most promising catalysts for ORR. However, Pt-based materials are not only high cost, but also scarce [2]. Hence, a large number of the cheap, highly active and long-time stable ORR catalysts are urgently needed [3].

Recently, the cobalt oxide (Co3O4) with reasonable ORR activity, easy preparation and abundant resources is reported as excellent alternatives to Pt-based catalysts [4]. Co3O4 has been widely proved as a stable ORR electrocatalyst in alkaline electrolyte [5]. However, the practical application of the cobalt oxide as catalysts for the ORR is limited by the relatively low density of active sites and poor electrical conductivity [6]. Consequently, it is critical to enhance the ORR performance and the electron transport of Co3O4. The introduction of carbon species, such as the graphene, carbon nanotubes and N-doped graphene, is an efficient way to improve the charge-transfer ability and the ORR performance. The application of Co3O4/carbon composite in electrocatalytic ORR has been widely reported. For example, Co3O4 nanocrystal/N-doped carbon nanotubes can be utilized as an effective bifunctional (such as ORR/OER) electrocatalyst for Zn-air batteries [7]. Co3O4 nanoparticle-decorated carbon nanofiber also can effectively catalyzes ORR via 4-electron transfer process and be utilized as an excellent air cathode of the Zn-air battery [8]. The hybrid material composed of Co3O4 nanocrystals and graphene layers exhibits excellent ORR activity [9]. Furthermore, zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) have attracted numerous attentions on account of their fast electron transfer ability, the large surface area and abundant surface groups [10]. CDs can be easy to composite with various functional materials, which is favorable for enhancing the catalytic activity of these functional materials. Yang’s group reported CDs-decorated cobaltous oxide nanoparticles with high-performance photo/electrocatalytic activities [11]. Inspired by this, the introduction of CDs into Co3O4 can improve the ORR catalytic performance of Co3O4.

Herein, the carbon dots/Co3O4 (CDs/Co3O4) catalyst was prepared through a simple in-situ two-step heating strategy. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and organic base of 1,8-diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene were used as raw materials. The carbon dots (CDs) were derived from the C source of g-C3N4, which were in situ introduced into Co3O4 to form the CDs/Co3O4 composite. The N element in g-C3N4 escaped in the form of NH3. The introduction of CDs endowed enhanced electrocatalytic activity of the CDs/Co3O4 composite. All of the electrocatalytic tests showed that the CDs/Co3O4 composite had excellent ORR catalytic activity, good anti-interference ability to methanol and stability.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, melamine, potassium hydroxide and ethanol (99.7%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 1,8-Diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene was obtained from Beijing Bailing Wei Technology Co., Ltd. Nafion solution (10 wt%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Acetylene black was obtained from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. All of the chemicals were utilized without further treatment.

Synthesis of g-C3N4: 2.0 g of melamine was transferred to a porcelain crucible with a lid, heated to 500 °C in muffle furnace under Ar with a ramp rate of 5 °C min−1 for 4 h. After cooling to the room temperature (≈25 °C), the sheet-structure g-C3N4 was obtained.

Synthesis of the CDs/Co3O4 catalyst: 0.04 g of g-C3N4 was added into 15 mL ultrapure water and processed via the ultrasonic treatment for 40 min. Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (0.1455 g) was then added into the above suspension and stirred for 10 min. Then, 1 mL organic alkali 1,8-Diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene was then added to the above solution by micropipette After being continuously stirred for 30 min, the solution was placed in a 25 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and kept at 180 °C. After for 12 h, the above Teflon-sealed autoclave was cooled down to the room temperature. Next, the precursor was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times. The obtained precursor was then dried at 50 °C for 24 h under vacuum. Finally, the precursor was put into a muffle furnace with a heating rate of 5 °C/min to reach 500 °C and maintained for 3 h. When cooled down sufficiently, the CDs/Co3O4 catalyst was collected after washed and dried. The Co3O4 sample was also prepared without g-C3N4 under the same condition.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F), field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7800F) were utilized to observe the morphology of as-synthesized samples. The crystal structures of catalysts were evaluated through X-ray diffraction (Shimadzu, XRD-6000X -ray) with high-intensity Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Avatar 470, Thermo Nicolet, FT-IR) was conducted through the standard KBr disk method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (PHI5300) with a monochromatic Mg Kα source.

Electrochemical performances were tested on CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument, USA) with a typical three-electrode system. An Ag/AgCl electrode (saturated KCl) and a graphite rod were used as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. A glassy-carbon electrode (GCE) (the diameter is 3 mm) was the working electrode.

The preparation of working electrodes: 3 mg of catalyst and 20 μL of 10 wt% Nafion solution were dispersed in 1000 μL of mixed solution with 1:1 water/ethanol and then sonicated for at least 1 h. Then, 10 μL of dispersed catalyst was dripped onto the GCE or RRDE.

The Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were carried out in O2 or N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH with the scan rate of 50 mV s−1. And linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were also recorded with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The capacitive current was eliminated. The chronoamperometry measurement was tested at 0.4 V with a rotation speed of 1600 rpm.

RRDE (Rotating Ring Disk Electrode, with the diameter of 4 nm) tests were performed at 1600 rpm. The ring electrode potential was set to be 1.25 V to oxidize HO2 − formed from the disk electrode. Moreover, the electron transfer number (n) and the HO2 − yield were calculated:
n = 4

where

is the disk current.

represents the ring current. And the

, the current collection efficiency of the Pt ring, is 0.37.

XRD patterns of the CDs/Co3O4, Co3O4 and g-C3N4 are shown in Fig. 1 (A). The diffraction peak positions of the CDs/Co3O4 are consistent with the standard peaks of Co3O4 (JCPDS No. 43-1003). The diffraction peaks indicate the good crystallinity of the Co3O4. Furthermore, the presence of any other impurity peaks is not observed, indicating that the prepared CDs/Co3O4 is pure phase. However, no characteristic diffraction peak of g-C3N4 is observed in the CDs/Co3O4, indicating that g-C3N4 is decomposed by high-temperature hydrothermal in an alkaline environment. The FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4, Co3O4, and the CDs/Co3O4 are displayed in Fig. 1(B). The g-C3N4 shows significant characteristic absorption peaks in the range of 810 cm−1, 887 cm−1 and 1200–1700 cm−1, corresponding to respiratory vibration peaks for the tris-S-triazine unit, the stretching vibration peaks for typical CN and CN heterocycles in the g-C3N4 [12]. Both of the Co3O4 and CDs/Co3O4 show significant absorption peaks at 566 cm−1 and 676 cm−1, which can be attributed to the characteristic peak of CoO bond in Co3O4 [13]. However, the characteristic absorption peaks of g-C3N4 are still not observed in the CDs/Co3O4, which is consistent with XRD results.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to analyze surface element composition and element valence of the Co3O4 and CDs/Co3O4. As shown in Fig. 1(C), the CDs/Co3O4 (a) and Co3O4 (b) have been detected the presence of Co, O and C. Co 2p3/2 can be split into two absorption peaks at positions of 781.6 eV and 780.1 eV, demonstrating the presence of Co2+ and Co3+ (Fig. 1D). The absorption peaks of O are divided into two characteristic peaks at 529.9 eV and 532.1 eV (Fig. 1E), corresponding to characteristic absorption peaks of lattice oxygen species and oxygen species, respectively. This result suggests that O element with the −2 valence state exists in the Co3O4 and CDs/Co3O4. Owing to the decomposition of the g-C3N4 after high temperature hydrothermal and calcination, no response signal of N element is detected in the CDs/Co3O4 (Fig. 1F) [14]. And, N species disappear by being converted into NH3 [15].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are utilized observe the apparent morphology of the Co3O4 and CDs/Co3O4. As shown in Fig. 2 (A) and (B), the Co3O4 and CDs/Co3O4 are nanoparticles (with the size of about 50 nm). The morphology of the Co3O4 and the CDs/Co3O4 are further analyzed by TEM, as shown in Fig. 2(E) and (F). Compared with the Co3O4 (Fig. 3 E), some little black dots exist in the surface of the CDs/Co3O4 (Fig. 2F), which are carbon dots. The carbon dots are derived from the C source of g-C3N4. After thermal treatment at 180 °C in a DBU alkaline environment and pyrolysis at 500 °C in a muffle furnace, the C element in g-C3N4 is in situ introduced into Co3O4 to form the CDs/Co3O4 composite. The N element in g-C3N4 escapes in the form of NH3.

Fig. 3(A) shows the Raman spectrum of CDs/Co3O4. The peak at 474 cm−1 can be assigned to Eg (the vibration of Co(II)O coordination), where the peaks at 514 cm−1 and 678 cm−1 are attributed to F2g and A1g (the vibration of Co(III)O coordination)[16]. The peaks at about 1349 cm−1 (D-band) and 1578 cm−1 (G-band) are ascribed to the CDs. The results proved the coexistence of carbon dots and Co3O4. The corresponding N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm (Fig. 3B) of CDs/Co3O4 has an IV type isotherm with hysteresis type H3, indicating the presence of mesopores piled up by carbon dots and Co3O4 nanoparticles. The BET specific surface area of sample is 155 m2·g−1 and the mesopore size is mostly located at 22 nm, which is in accord with the TEM results.

To evaluate the ORR performance of CDs/Co3O4 and Co3O4, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests are carried out in a range of +0.2 V to −0.8 V with a positive scan rate of 50 mV/s in N2-saturated and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. The potential and current density of the O2 reduction peak in the CV curves are used to evaluate the ORR activity of samples. The more positive starting potential and the higher current density, the better ORR activity. Obviously, the CV curves show that the CDs/Co3O4 and Co3O4 possess palpable peak current in O2-saturated electrolyte (Fig. 4 (A) and (B)), which shows that the CDs/Co3O4 and Co3O4 can catalyze ORR. In addition, the current density of the CDs/Co3O4 (0.85 mA/cm2) is higher than that of Co3O4 (0.68 mA/cm2), indicating that the CDs/Co3O4 has better ORR electrocatalytic activity than Co3O4. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of CDs/Co3O4 and Co3O4 are tested in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH with a scan rate of 10 mV/s. Similar to the CV results, the limiting current of the CDs/Co3O4 is 4.89 mA/cm2, higher than that of Co3O4 (4.3 mA/cm2) in LSV (Fig. 4C). The half-wave potential of CDs/Co3O4 is 0.6 V (vs. RHE), which equal to Co3O4. Fig. 4D shows that the current density of CDs/Co3O4 enhances with the increase of rotation rate. The above experimental results show that the introduction of carbon dots can improve the conductivity of Co3O4 materials and accelerate the electron transfer during ORR, further enhancing ORR electrocatalytic activity.

In order to determine the electron transfer number (n) and the hydrogen peroxide yield (%) of CDs/Co3O4 in the process of ORR, the ring current (Iring) and disk current (Idisk) are measured by rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH (Fig. 4E). According to the Iring and Idisk, the n and hydrogen peroxide yield (%) in ORR process are calculated (Fig. 4F). The n of the CDs/Co3O4 is calculated to be approximately 3.8 at −0.4 V to −0.8 V. The hydrogen peroxide yield (%) of the CDs/Co3O4 is less than 15% at −0.4 V to −0.8 V. The results show that a direct 4e− mechanism can dominate the ORR process of the CDs/Co3O4 in the alkaline electrolyte, indicating that the catalyst exhibits excellent ORR performance. The anti-methanol test of the CDs/Co3O4 shows that O2 reduction peaks are consistent before and after methanol injecting, indicating that the CDs/Co3O4 has excellent methanol tolerance (Fig. 4G). The chronoamperometric current response of the CDs/Co3O4 is tested, as shown Fig. 4H. After 6000 s with the rotating speed of 1600 rpm, the residual current retention rate of the CDs/Co3O4 is over 90%, indicating that the CDs/Co3O4 exhibits excellent ORR catalytic stability.

The CDs/Co3O4 has been synthesized through a simple in-situ two-step strategy. The CDs are derived from the C source of g-C3N4, which are in situ introduced into Co3O4 to form CDs/Co3O4 composite. The CDs/Co3O4 composite is evaluated the ORR activity in 0.1 M KOH. The electrochemical tests indicate the efficient effects of the CDs/Co3O4 composite on ORR activity via a four-electron process. In addition, the CDs/Co3O4 composite possesses good anti-interference ability to methanol and stability. Hence, the CDs/Co3O4 composite has been evidenced to be a good electrocatalyst for ORR, and can be applied in alkaline fuel cells.

Xiaoyan Zhang: Writing - original draft. Xiumei Cheng: Formal analysis. Feng Chen: Funding acquisition. Li Xu: Investigation, Supervision. Yaping Wang: Software. Junchao Qian: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Zhengying Wu: Resources. Qi Zhang: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been financially supported by Six Talent Peaks Project of Jiangsu Province (XNY-009), The Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grants No. BK20180971, BK20180103); Chinese Postdoctoral Foundation (2018T110450), Science and Technology Development Project of Suzhou (SYG201742, SYG201818); High-tech Research Key laboratory of Zhenjiang (SS2018002) and a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions; Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Environment Functional Materials (SJHG1802).
